brand.name: Swift Financial Group, LLC
brand.vertical: B2B/professional — financial services (seller-financed mortgage note buying)

summary: Swift Financial Group buys seller-financed (owner-financed) mortgage notes, converting a note holder's stream of future monthly payments into a single lump-sum cash payment at no cost to the seller. It serves individuals who financed a property sale themselves — retirees, small landlords, real estate investors, and heirs — who want immediate liquidity instead of waiting years to collect, including partial-note purchases for those who want some cash now and the note back later.

campaigns.name: Welcome & Quote Onboarding
campaigns.objective: Convert a fresh quote inquiry into an engaged, trusting prospect by setting expectations and delivering the first reassurance win before they go cold or shop competitors.
campaigns.trigger: Quote request submitted (online form or phone) — send within seconds.
campaigns.segment: New note holders who just requested a quote; branch later by stated reason for selling (retirement/estate, investor, heir, first-time seller).
campaigns.rationale: New quote requesters currently get a number and silence — the single biggest conversion leak. An instant, plain-language welcome that explains the simple, cost-free process builds trust at the exact moment doubt sets in.

emails.purpose: Instant warm welcome: confirm the request is received, set expectations for the offer timeline, and deliver the first 'win' — the relief that the process is simple and costs them nothing.
emails.subject: Your note quote is on its way — here's what happens next
emails.preview: No cost, no obligation. Here's exactly how we turn your monthly payments into a lump sum.
keyPoints: Confirm we received their note details and a no-obligation cash offer is being prepared (same-day to a few days)
Walk the 4 simple steps: get your quote, accept, we handle due diligence and paperwork, you get paid — Swift covers all closing costs
Reassure with 20+ years in business and that they are never obligated to proceed; a real person reads every reply
Set the relationship: a few short, helpful emails ahead — no pressure, just clarity

campaigns.name: Quote Follow-Up & Conversion
campaigns.objective: Move a delivered-but-unaccepted quote to acceptance by dismantling the #1 objection ('I'll lose too much selling at a discount') and reinforcing simplicity and speed.
campaigns.trigger: Offer sent but not accepted after 3–7 days; runs in parallel with a light urgency/expiry nudge.
campaigns.segment: Prospects who received a cash offer and have not responded; branch by note size and stated reason for selling.
campaigns.rationale: This is the core revenue play and the highest-leverage gap — quote requesters go cold with no follow-up. The discount objection is the make-or-break unlock, and only education on time-value and risk transfer overcomes it.

emails.purpose: Reframe the discount objection: show why a lump sum today can beat years of monthly payments once risk, inflation, and the time value of money are accounted for — then make accepting feel safe and easy.
emails.subject: Worried the discount means losing money? Here's the real math
emails.preview: Cash today vs. 20 years of payments — what the discount actually buys you.
keyPoints: Name the fear directly: a lump sum looks smaller than the total of all future payments — here's why that comparison is misleading
Explain time-value plainly: money now is worth more than money spread over decades, and waiting carries real default and inflation risk you currently absorb
The discount transfers that borrower-default risk and collection burden to Swift — you trade uncertainty for certainty
Low-pressure close: your offer stands with no obligation, and a partial sale is available if you want some cash now and the note back later
Include standard disclosure: offers vary by note terms; not a guarantee

campaigns.name: Quoted-But-Didn't-Sell Win-Back
campaigns.objective: Reactivate warm leads who got a quote but never closed, by tying a fresh offer to changing life circumstances and current rates.
campaigns.trigger: 90+ days of inactivity after a quote that didn't convert; re-run on a 3–6 month cadence.
campaigns.segment: Dormant prior quote recipients who still hold their note; suppress anyone who already sold.
campaigns.rationale: People who got a quote and didn't move forward are the warmest cold audience this brand has, and there is no re-engagement in place. Life events (retirement, health, an investment opportunity, a buyer falling behind) change readiness — a low-cost periodic touch recovers 5–15%.

emails.purpose: Reopen the conversation without pressure: remind them of the core value, acknowledge that circumstances change, and offer a fresh, updated quote.
emails.subject: Still holding that mortgage note?
emails.preview: Life changes — and so can your offer. Get a fresh, no-obligation quote in minutes.
keyPoints: Gentle re-open: it's been a while — your note, and your reasons for keeping it, may look different now
Surface the triggers that change the math: retirement, medical or tuition costs, an estate to settle, a buyer who's slowing down on payments, or a new opportunity needing cash
Offer is free and updated to today's terms; partial sale still available if full feels like too much
Reassure: still no cost to you, still no obligation, still the same 20+ year team — just reply or click to get a new number

campaigns.name: "Should You Sell Your Note?" Educational Nurture
campaigns.objective: Move fence-sitting note holders toward readiness by educating them on when selling makes sense, building authority and trust over a longer cycle.
campaigns.trigger: Download of the decision guide / educational lead magnet ('Should You Sell Your Mortgage Note?').
campaigns.segment: Leads who downloaded the guide but are below sales-ready intent; branch by reason for selling and note size if known.
campaigns.rationale: The brand has no educational content and no top-of-funnel asset beyond the quote, so undecided prospects have nothing to engage with between curiosity and commitment. A decision-guide nurture captures earlier-stage holders and warms them before they ever request a quote.

emails.purpose: Deliver the downloaded guide and frame the core decision honestly — that selling isn't always right, which is exactly what builds the trust that makes them choose Swift when it is.
emails.subject: Should you sell your mortgage note? A straight answer
emails.preview: The 5 questions every note holder should ask before selling — and when waiting costs you.
keyPoints: Deliver the guide link up front and set an honest frame: selling isn't right for everyone — here's how to tell
Preview the 5 questions: Do you need liquidity now? How solid is your buyer? How much management is it costing you? What would the cash unlock? Full or partial sale?
Establish authority with plain-language teaching, not a pitch — 20+ years buying notes
Soft next step: when you want to see what your specific note is worth, a free no-obligation quote is one click away

campaigns.name: Post-Close Referral & Partial-to-Full Expansion
campaigns.objective: Turn satisfied note sellers into an acquisition channel and surface repeat business from partial-note clients and multi-note investors.
campaigns.trigger: Close completed (lump-sum funded); for partial sales, a follow-up touch as the retained note matures.
campaigns.segment: Recently funded clients at their peak-satisfaction moment; branch investors/multi-note holders and partial-sale clients separately.
campaigns.rationale: Referrals from sellers, real estate attorneys, CPAs, and agents are explicitly the highest-potential untapped channel, and there's no referral program at all. The just-funded moment — when the client has the cash in hand — is the strongest possible time to ask, and partial-sale clients are a built-in repeat path.

emails.purpose: Capitalize on the peak-value moment after funding to request a warm referral and remind partial-sale clients that Swift can buy the rest of the note later.
emails.subject: You've got your cash — know someone else holding a note?
emails.preview: Most note holders know another. A quick intro could put cash in their hands too.
keyPoints: Celebrate the close: you have all your money now and the waiting is over — congratulations
Make the ask natural: note holders tend to know others (neighbors who owner-financed, family, attorneys, agents, fellow investors) who'd value the same relief
Frame referral as a favor to them, not a sales pitch — a simple intro or forward is all it takes
For partial-sale clients: a reminder that when you're ready to cash out the remaining payments, we're here to buy the rest
Keep it warm and low-pressure, consistent with a trust-first, 20+ year brand
